title: Testing, Release Gates, and Memory Incident Defense · Pointer Forensics
summary: An incident responder inherits a corrupted production memory-safety defense dossier, the earliest failing input, and two plausible hypotheses; they must preserve first-failure evidence, repair the cause, and add a regression. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in production memory-safety defense dossier and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defens…
objectives: Combine unit, property, integration, fault, stress, sanitizer, fuzz, ABI, resource, load, and restore evidence by risk.
Test supported GCC and Clang versions, optimization modes, architectures, libraries, feature macros, and warning baselines.
Pin source, dependencies, compiler, flags, generated files, symbols, SBOM, provenance, and artifact identity.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug Memory Test Layers
steps.instruction:
debug case — A debug native-systems team handles memory case 8245. Produce bounded C evidence to decide release readiness from complementary memory evidence; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Memory Incident Response
steps.instruction:
For this debug demonstration of cmem-memory-incident-response, write the complete compile-ready C program headed "/* Evidence: cmem-memory-incident-response-1nnzrn */" for changed case 1638. It must decide release readiness from complementary memory evidence, reject one changed boundary, print an observable result and invariant, and return nonzero when the invariant fails. Keep instant code within the PicoC 3.2.2 practice subset; do not claim C23, optimizing compiler, sanitizer, ABI, thread, operating-system, FFI, load, or production evidence until its explicit controlled transfer gate passes.

hints: For Publish the incident record · Debug Memory Incident Response, name the size, bounds, object, lifetime, owner, borrow, allocation, cleanup, representation, ABI, thread, and transfer boundary, Memory Incident Response precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Publish the incident record · Debug Memory Incident Response, trace this concrete input and predict size arithmetic, pointer range, lifetime transition, allocation result, cleanup order, printed output, and native evidence still missing before reviewing it: A debug native-systems team handles memory case 4293. Produce bounded C evidence to decide release readiness from complementary memory evidence; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Preserve first-failure artifacts, symbolize safely, contain exposure, reproduce, repair causally, and verify recovery and prevention.

For Publish the incident record · Debug Memory Incident Response, challenge the documented misconception with changed-size, failure-injection, printed invariant, compiler, sanitizer, analyzer, fuzz, ABI, concurrency, or production-transfer evidence: Restarting the process and adding memory is sufficient incident resolution.
